quiz.title: Geometry: Space Figures and Nets
quiz.subtitle: Unit 11 Quiz 1: Identifying 3D Figures and Their 2D Representations (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: A net of a solid is defined as:
options: A two-dimensional arrangement of polygons that folds into a three-dimensional solid
A three-dimensional skeletal framework that outlines the edges of a solid figure
A two-dimensional cross-section produced by intersecting a solid with a cutting plane
A two-dimensional orthographic projection showing only the top face of a solid
questions.explanation: A net is a 2D pattern made of polygons that can be folded along its edges to form the faces of a 3D solid. Each face of the solid corresponds to one polygon in the net. Not every arrangement of faces forms a valid net — the faces must connect correctly when folded.

questions.question: Which solid is represented by a net consisting of six congruent squares?
questions.explanation: A cube has six congruent square faces. Its net consists of six squares arranged in a cross or other valid pattern. A rectangular prism also has six rectangular faces, but they are not all congruent unless it is a cube.

questions.question: A net contains one square and four congruent triangles. Which solid does it form when folded?
questions.explanation: A square pyramid has a square base and four triangular lateral faces that meet at a common vertex (the apex). The net places the four triangles around the square base. A triangular pyramid would have four triangular faces and no square.

questions.question: How many faces does a triangular prism have?
questions.explanation: A triangular prism has two triangular bases and three rectangular lateral faces, for a total of $2 + 3 = 5$ faces. Its net consists of two triangles and three rectangles.

questions.question: Which of the following nets will NOT fold into a cube?
options: Six squares arranged in a straight line (1 by 6)
Six squares in a cross shape (a central square with four attached to its sides and one more attached to one of those four)
Six squares in a T-shape with specific attachments
Six squares in a staircase-like arrangement
questions.explanation: Six squares in a straight line cannot fold into a cube because when folded, multiple faces would overlap. There are exactly 11 distinct nets that fold into a cube. Common invalid arrangements include a 1×6 strip, a 2×3 rectangle with no extra squares, and patterns where four squares meet at a point.

questions.question: A solid has a net consisting of two congruent hexagons and six rectangles. What is the solid?
questions.explanation: A hexagonal prism has two hexagonal bases and six rectangular lateral faces (one for each side of the hexagon). Total faces: $2 + 6 = 8$. The net has two hexagons and six rectangles — perfectly matching this description.

questions.question: How many vertices does a rectangular prism have?
questions.explanation: A rectangular prism has $8$ vertices — four on the top base and four on the bottom base. It also has $6$ rectangular faces and $12$ edges. These numbers satisfy Euler's Formula: $V - E + F = 8 - 12 + 6 = 2$.

questions.question: A net shows two congruent triangles and three rectangles. Which solid is formed?
questions.explanation: A triangular prism has two triangular bases and three rectangular lateral faces connecting corresponding sides of the triangles. The net places the three rectangles between or around the two triangles. Total: $5$ faces, $6$ vertices, $9$ edges.

questions.question: Which statement about a cylinder is true?
options: A cylinder has three flat polygonal faces that intersect at sharp vertices
A cylinder's net consists of two circles and a single isosceles triangle
A cylinder has two congruent circular bases connected by a curved lateral surface
A cylinder has four vertices where the circular edges meet the lateral face
questions.explanation: A cylinder has two congruent circular bases connected by a curved lateral surface. Its net consists of two circles and a rectangle, not a triangle. Because it contains a curved surface, it is not a polyhedron, and it has zero vertices, not four.

questions.question: A cone's net consists of which shapes?
options: A circle and a sector of a circle
questions.explanation: A cone has a circular base and a curved lateral surface that tapers to a point (the apex). Its net consists of one circle (the base) and a sector of a larger circle (the lateral surface unrolled). The sector's radius equals the slant height of the cone, and its arc length equals the circumference of the base.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You can confidently identify 3D solids from their nets, count faces/vertices/edges, and distinguish prisms from pyramids. You understand the relationship between 2D patterns and the 3D figures they form.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) A net is a 2D pattern that folds into a 3D solid, (2) Prisms have two congruent bases; pyramids have one base and triangular faces meeting at an apex, (3) Cylinders and cones have curved surfaces — their nets include circles and rectangles or sectors.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) prisms are named by their base shape (triangular prism = triangle bases + rectangles), (2) pyramids have one base and triangular lateral faces, (3) counting faces, vertices, and edges — use Euler's Formula $V - E + F = 2$, (4) nets for curved solids (cylinder, cone) include circles and curved surfaces unrolled.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Key solids: Cube (6 square faces), rectangular prism (6 rectangular faces), triangular prism (2 triangles + 3 rectangles), square pyramid (1 square + 4 triangles), cylinder (2 circles + 1 rectangle), cone (1 circle + 1 sector). A net must fold without overlapping faces. Euler's Formula: $V - E + F = 2$ for all polyhedra.
